critic_findings.message: No main()-success-path test. The file covers 2 error-exit branches and 1 skip branch but the claimed 'main() stdout' branch is absent. Add a test that builds a minimal agents+skills fixture, calls amt.main(), asserts rc==0 and stdout contains expected output.

_fmt_tokens and _fmt_cost are duplicated across token_report_skills.py and token_report_render.py with differing implementations (abbreviated M/k vs raw integer; zero-handling differs). Extract to a shared token_report_fmt.py utility and import from both.

findings_details.title: _hook_now_iso has no fallback for bash <4.2
findings_details.body: _hook_now_iso uses printf '%(%Y-%m-%dT%H:%M:%SZ)T' -1, which requires bash 4.2+. macOS system bash is 3.2.57 (verified: /bin/bash at /bin/bash). When hooks are dispatched with #!/usr/bin/env bash and the user's PATH resolves to /bin/bash (default on macOS without Homebrew bash), the printf builtin emits 'printf: (: invalid format character' to stderr AND produces no output. This produces (a) dispatcher-stderr noise surfaced to the harness as hook-error row, and (b) perf rows with ts='' (empty), corrupting the JSONL log. _hook_now_ms has a perl fallback for this exact portability concern; _hook_now_iso needs one too. Fix: add a fallback using 'date -u +%Y-%m-%dT%H:%M:%SZ' when printf builtin fails, or check BASH_VERSINFO[0] -ge 4 at source time and pick the impl once.

findings_details.title: Budget-key example uses wrong dispatcher suffix
findings_details.body: Example on lines 33-37 shows keys like 'PreToolUse.rq-bash-pre-dispatcher.gate-issue-binding.sh'. Actual dispatcher field (see HOOK_DISPATCHER_NAME in rq-bash-pre-dispatcher.sh line 31 and hook-budgets.json) is 'rq-bash-pre' with no '-dispatcher' suffix. Users copy-pasting this example will see zero budget matches in check_budgets. Fix: drop the '-dispatcher' suffix in both example keys.

findings_details.title: dispatcher field description names '-dispatcher.sh' suffix
findings_details.body: Line 12 of the schema table describes the dispatcher field as 'Dispatcher script name without path (e.g. rq-bash-pre-dispatcher.sh)'. The actual value written is the HOOK_DISPATCHER_NAME export ('rq-bash-pre', 'rq-bash-post', 'rq-prompt-submit', 'rq-session') — no '.sh' or '-dispatcher' suffix. Fix: change the example to 'rq-bash-pre'.

findings_details.title: Docs claim 'bin/hook-perf' shows last 50 rows by default — actually shows all rows
findings_details.body: Line 22: 'bin/hook-perf — plain table, last 50 rows.' But hook_perf_summary.py (scripts/hook_perf_summary.py) defines --last with default=None, and load_rows only truncates 'if limit is not None and limit > 0'. With no flag, all rows are loaded and summarized. There is no built-in 50-row default anywhere. Fix: either change the default in main() to 50, or correct the docs to say 'all rows (use --last N to restrict)'.

findings_details.title: --json flag described as 'raw JSONL passthrough' — actually emits summary JSON
findings_details.body: Line 23: 'bin/hook-perf --json — raw JSONL passthrough (pipe to jq).' In hook_perf_summary.py main() (line 199-201), --json prints json.dumps(summary, indent=2) where summary is the aggregated per-key dict (count/p50/p95/max/fails). It is NOT raw JSONL passthrough of the source log. Fix: describe it as 'aggregated summary as JSON (for machine consumption)' — matching the argparse help text on line 183.

findings_details.title: Plain-table column headings list is wrong
findings_details.body: Line 26: 'Column headings in the plain table: event, dispatcher, sub_guard, duration_ms, exit_code.' The actual print_table header (hook_perf_summary.py line 121) is: event, dispatcher, sub_guard, count, p50, p95, max, fails. The doc lists per-row raw log fields instead of the aggregated table output. Fix: replace with the actual column list.

findings_details.title: Stale performance description contradicts post-#417 baseline
findings_details.body: Lines 41: 'The current floor for most sub-guards is roughly 240–316 ms, dominated by python3 startup overhead inside hook_run_guard (three invocations per sub-guard...). A p95_ms of 600 ms gives ~2× headroom over that floor...' This is the pre-#417 baseline prose. Post-#417 (documented in hook-perf-baseline.md line 41-47 and README line 33-35), the p95 is 15–36 ms and budgets are tightened to 100–500 ms, not 600 ms. Fix: rewrite this paragraph to reflect the post-#417 state, or delete it since hook-perf-baseline.md already covers it.

findings_details.title: 'Optimization follow-up' section references issue #417 as open — it is this PR
findings_details.body: Line 51-53: 'Optimization follow-up — Known bottleneck: python3 cold start ... Tracked at https://github.com/arqu-co/agent-plugins/issues/417.' PR #402 (this PR) has folded in #417 per the review context; the bash-native hot path rewrite is now shipping. hook-perf-baseline.md already documents completion. This section is stale and confuses readers. Fix: either reframe as 'Completed in #417 (merged with #402)' or delete the section.

findings_details.title: Baseline table labeled 'N=10' while reproduce instructions and default are N=30
findings_details.body: Line 3: 'Generated by ... (N=30 iterations...)'. Line 8-9 reproduce block: 'HOOK_BENCH_ITERATIONS=30 bash ...'. hook-perf-benchmark.sh line 21: 'ITERATIONS=${HOOK_BENCH_ITERATIONS:-30}'. But line 11 header says 'Table (N=10, stub sub-guards, post-#417 bash-native hot path)' and every count column in the table shows 10. Either the baseline was regenerated with N=10 and lines 3/8 are wrong, or the table is from an old run and N=30 rerun is needed. Fix: make the documented N consistent across the file — if baseline was captured at N=10, update lines 3 and 8; if the canonical N is 30, regenerate the table at N=30.

findings_details.title: track-session-prs sub-guard never exercised by benchmark matrix
findings_details.body: The matrix (lines 128-162) runs 11 scenarios covering drift, gate-issue-binding, gate-skill-isolation, enforce-markdown-staged, require-critic-approval, run-gates-all, enforce-review-before-pr, enforce-tokens-posted, enforce-gh-body-markdown, block-merge-without-ci, enforce-fixes, consume-critic-approval, enforce-spec-posted, enforce-plan-posted, hook-trace-reinstall, and the three SessionStart sub-guards. But the PostToolUse:Bash dispatcher routes track-session-prs on any 'gh pr ...' command (rq-bash-post-dispatcher.sh lines 28, 71) — no benchmark scenario fires a 'gh pr ...' command through POST_DISPATCHER. hook-budgets.json line 18 defines a 100ms p95 budget for 'PostToolUse.rq-bash-post.track-session-prs' that is unverified by the harness. check_budgets silently skips entries with no measured p95, so a regression would slip past. Fix: add scenario 'l. gh pr create (post)' that pipes _bash_post_payload 'gh pr create --title x' to POST_DISPATCHER, OR document the intentional gap in the baseline file.

findings_details.title: check_budgets silently skips budget keys with no measured data
findings_details.body: check_budgets (lines 151-162) iterates summary.items() and compares against budgets. Budget keys with NO matching summary entry are never checked — no warning, no non-zero exit. If hook-budgets.json declares a p95 budget for a sub-guard that never fires in a benchmark run, the --budget check passes silently. This matches the comment but is hazardous for a regression gate. Fix: iterate budgets.items() instead (or in addition), and print a 'no data for budget key X' warning so benchmark coverage gaps surface. Optional: make missing-data a soft warning that does not fail the run, but is visible.

findings_details.title: --last accepts negative values and silently slices from the wrong end
findings_details.body: load_rows (line 59-60): 'if limit is not None and limit > 0: rows = rows[-limit:]'. Guard rejects limit<=0, but the argparse option --last uses type=int with no floor. User passing '--last -5' parses fine, limit=-5, the guard trips (not >0), and load_rows returns all rows — not 'last -5 rows', which doesn't have a meaningful interpretation anyway. Mildly surprising: there is no error message or warning. Fix: either make --last argparse type a positive-int function (rejecting <=0), or print a warning 'ignoring non-positive --last' before returning all rows.
